cross_scene_arcs.title: The Control of Narrative: From Subject to Author
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Andy evolves from being a passive subject of narrative to an active author who controls how his story is told, both in media and performance. This arc traces his increasing determination to dictate the terms of his artistic expression and public perception.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Andy is misrepresented in a biopic and feels powerless over his public image.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Andy subverts audience expectations by replacing live performance with a pre-recorded reading, asserting control over reception and format.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: The tension between authentic self-expression and performative manipulation remains unresolved, as audiences and allies struggle to discern truth from artifice.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Father's Gaze and the Burden of Validation
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Andy's early conflict with his father over the legitimacy of his imaginative performances echoes throughout his life, manifesting in his need to provoke real emotional reactions and test the boundaries of authenticity, especially within familial relationships.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Andy's father invalidates his private performances, demanding real audiences and external validation.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Andy stages a fake injury that deeply affects his mother and enrages his father, reenacting the emotional stakes of seeking recognition while risking familial trust.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Whether Andy seeks genuine emotional connection or merely performance-induced reactions from his family remains ambiguous, perpetuating relational instability.

cross_scene_arcs.title: Artistic Integrity vs. Commercial Success
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Andy repeatedly confronts the tension between staying true to his unconventional artistic vision and the pressures of mainstream entertainment, leading to cycles of rejection, sabotage, and reluctant compromise.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Andy performs a childlike act to an indifferent adult audience and is fired, facing early professional rejection.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Andy quits 'Taxi' in protest but remains contractually bound, forced to return under a new persona, symbolizing his entrapment in the system he resists.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Andy's inability to fully escape commercial structures undermines his anti-establishment stance, leaving his artistic freedom perpetually constrained.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Tony Clifton Masquerade: Identity, Power, and Betrayal
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: The creation, negotiation, and unmasking of Tony Clifton serve as a central vehicle for Andy to explore identity, manipulate power dynamics, and test loyalty, culminating in emotional fallout and blurred lines between reality and performance.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Andy introduces Tony Clifton as a non-negotiable condition for joining a sitcom, asserting control through absurdity.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Andy reveals Tony as his own creation, then later stages a fake firing and improvisation, demonstrating complete command over narrative and perception.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: The extent to which Andy's manipulations are artistic experiments or emotional abuses remains unclear, eroding trust with key allies like George and Ed.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Collapse of Trust in the Face of Truth
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: As Andy's deceptions escalate, the line between performance and reality becomes so porous that even genuine suffering is met with skepticism, culminating in a crisis of belief when he reveals a real, life-threatening illness.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Andy fakes a severe injury, successfully eliciting real emotions but destroying trust with his family.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Andy discloses his actual lung cancer diagnosis, but is met with doubt from his closest collaborators, who believe it may be another stunt.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Andy's past performances have made it impossible for others to believe his truth, leaving him isolated at the moment he most needs support.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Art of Deception: Sustaining the Tony Clifton Myth
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Bob Zmuda evolves from a behind-the-scenes collaborator to the primary steward of the Tony Clifton persona, actively maintaining and extending the performance art hoax long after Andy Kaufman's death. This arc traces the deliberate construction and perpetuation of ambiguity around Clifton's identity as a core artistic goal.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Zmuda is revealed as a co-creator of the Tony Clifton ruse, working with Andy to destabilize George's perception of authenticity.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Zmuda reactivates the Tony Clifton character one year after Andy's death, suggesting the performance continues beyond Andy's physical presence.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: It remains unclear whether the resurrection of Tony Clifton is a tribute, a continuation of Andy's vision, or Zmuda's own artistic appropriation.

cross_scene_arcs.title: Protector and Provocateur: Zmuda's Shifting Role in Andy's Journey
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Zmuda transitions from artistic collaborator to emotional caretaker and protector, navigating moments of Andy's vulnerability—from stress and performance anxiety to terminal illness—while struggling with his own emotional limits and loyalty.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Zmuda defends Andy's artistic autonomy against external pressures, positioning himself as a guardian of creative integrity.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Zmuda is emotionally overwhelmed as Andy faces death with calm detachment, revealing a growing emotional rift and Zmuda's deep personal investment.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Zmuda's role shifts from equal partner to dependent supporter, leaving his sense of identity and purpose uncertain after Andy's passing.

cross_scene_arcs.title: From Subversion to Legacy: The Evolution of Performance Ethics
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Zmuda repeatedly confronts the ethical boundaries of performance art, advocating for increasingly extreme stunts—from manipulating audiences to exploiting Andy's illness—sparking internal and external conflicts about the cost of artistic provocation.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Zmuda convinces Andy to stay on the show by reframing it as a platform for subversion, embracing mass media as a tool for deception.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Zmuda proposes using Andy's cancer as comedic material, alienating others and exposing a moral rift between artistic ambition and human empathy.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: The group never resolves whether their art transcends cruelty or becomes indistinguishable from it, leaving Zmuda's provocations ethically ambiguous.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Final Performance: Faith, Fraud, and Catharsis
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: Zmuda witnesses the collapse of hope for Andy's physical healing, transitions into mourning, and then reignites the mythos through the reappearance of Tony Clifton—blurring faith, grief, and performance into a final act that questions the nature of truth and legacy.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: Zmuda shares Andy's hope for a miracle cure in the Philippines, believing in the possibility of redemption through alternative healing.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: Zmuda perpetuates the ambiguity of Andy's death by reviving Tony Clifton, suggesting the performance has become a religion of its own.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Whether Zmuda believes in the art as spiritual truth or merely sustains it as a construct remains unknown, leaving his motivations and beliefs unresolved.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Tony Clifton Deception and George's Erosion of Control
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: George's professional and psychological journey begins with a threatening call from Tony Clifton and culminates in the shattering realization that Tony is a persona of Andy Kaufman. This arc traces George's growing confusion, investigative efforts, and ultimate disillusionment as the boundary between performance and reality collapses.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: George is warned to stay away from Andy by the hostile Tony Clifton, whom he believes to be a separate, disruptive figure.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: George confronts the emptiness of Andy's art after fully grasping the Tony Clifton ruse, leading to skepticism about the ethical cost of the performance.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Whether Andy's deceptions serve a higher artistic purpose or are merely self-indulgent pranks remains unresolved, straining George's loyalty.

cross_scene_arcs.title: George as Mediator and Enforcer of Artistic Boundaries
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: George evolves from a passive manager into an active gatekeeper of Andy's career, navigating production crises, public backlash, and personal loyalty. This arc follows his attempts to balance artistic freedom with commercial viability and professional accountability.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: George seeks to align Andy's performances with audience expectations while preserving creative space.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: George successfully brokers Andy's return to SNL by framing his apology as redemption, marking a high point in his rehabilitative efforts.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Despite George's interventions, Andy remains emotionally detached and resistant to guidance, raising doubts about the sustainability of his career recovery.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Fracturing Relationship: Trust, Art, and Ethics
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: This arc charts the deterioration of George and Andy's relationship as philosophical and ethical disagreements over comedy, authenticity, and illness deepen. From professional concern to moral confrontation, George increasingly distances himself from Andy's choices.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: George expresses alarm over public backlash to Andy's Merv Griffin appearance, hoping to realign his satire with audience understanding.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: George openly challenges the authenticity of Andy's illness and healing practices, exposing a profound rift in values and trust.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: The authenticity of Andy's suffering and his use of performance as a shield remain ambiguous, leaving George emotionally alienated despite lingering care.

cross_scene_arcs.title: The Return and Final Performance: Reality or Illusion?
cross_scene_arcs.arc_summary: After Andy's apparent death, George is thrust into a surreal arc where he confronts signs of Andy's return, culminating in a final performance that blurs life, death, and art. This arc blends mystery, emotional reckoning, and unresolved questions about legacy.
cross_scene_arcs.start_state: George sees Andy's name on a marquee, triggering disbelief and cognitive dissonance about his death.
cross_scene_arcs.end_state: George witnesses Andy's funeral as a final performance, accepting that Andy's life and death are inseparable from his art.
cross_scene_arcs.unresolved_issue: Whether Andy truly died, faked his death, or exists now only as a myth remains ambiguous, denying George full closure.
